serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of vegetable briyani in Singapore is approximately 300 g (one plate), providing around 387 kcal, 11.6 g protein, 12.5 g fat, and 57.4 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Nasi Briyani (Vegetable) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Nasi Briyani (Vegetable), CR ID F05100082. All seven reported nutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are sourced at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous vegetable biryani entry (USDA SR Legacy #21052) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this item.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for Singaporean vegetable briyani. GI of 56 estimated from University of Sydney GI Database entry for basmati rice-based biryani (closest analogous food, GI ~56–58 range, medium category). Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as: (56 × 19.14 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 3) / 100 = 32.2 / 3 ≈ 10.7 per 100 g equivalent serving basis. GL per serving (300 g) = (56 × 57.42) / 100 = 32.2. Reported GL per serving = 32.2; per-100g GL = 10.7. Input record GI of 15 (low) was rejected as unsupported and inconsistent with basmati rice-based dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 339 mg/100 g with limited potassium offset (~160 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 339 mg/100 g is borderline high for hypertensive individuals; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 1,017 mg sodium (44% of the 2,300 mg daily limit), and the modest potassium content (~480 mg per serving) is insufficient to offset this load — patients should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion size to 150–200 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 19.1 g/100 g with low dietary fibre (1.42 g/100 g) and medium GI (~56)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The medium GI (~56) and low fibre-to-carbohydrate ratio (1.42 g fibre per 19.1 g carbohydrate) result in moderate glycaemic impact; individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit portions to 150–200 g, pair with a high-fibre legume side such as dal, and choose preparations using less ghee to reduce overall energy density.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~1.8 g/100 g from ghee) and elevated sodium (339 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Vegetable briyani has a relatively low saturated fat content (~1.8 g/100 g) and no trans fat, making it acceptable for cardiovascular health in moderate portions; however, the sodium content of 339 mg/100 g warrants caution for patients with concurrent hypertension, and ghee quantity should be minimised to keep saturated fat below 10% of total energy.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (cinnamon, cardamom, star anise) and mixed vegetables with antioxidants
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The anti-inflammatory spice profile (cinnamon, cardamom, star anise) and mixed vegetable content support gut-immune modulation; patients with nightshade sensitivity should monitor tomato tolerance, and ghee quantity should be minimised to limit saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasi Briyani (Vegetable) is a fragrant Singaporean-Indian rice dish in which long-grain basmati rice is cooked with ghee and a blend of whole spices — cinnamon, cardamom, and star anise — then combined with fried mixed vegetables including carrot, french bean, pea, corn, onion, and tomato. The dish is a vegetarian adaptation of the classic South Asian biryani tradition and is widely served at Indian-Muslim hawker stalls and catered events across Singapore. The texture is fluffy and slightly oily from the ghee, with a warm, aromatic spice profile and a mildly sweet vegetable undertone. Per 100 g, it provides 129 kcal, 3.86 g protein, 4.15 g fat (approximately 1.8 g saturated), 19.14 g carbohydrate, 1.42 g dietary fibre, 0.51 g sugar, and 339 mg sodium. The medium glycemic index (~56) and moderate carbohydrate load make portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The sodium content is borderline high, requiring caution for hypertensive individuals. The dish is halal, vegetarian, and gluten-free, making it suitable for a broad range of dietary requirements. It is commonly consumed as a working lunch or festive meal in Singapore.

GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous basmati rice biryani entry. All mandatory multilingual names present. Ontology anchors are defensible.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this vegetable briyani preparation is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for an exact match as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in HPB source for this item. calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient data not available in HPB Singapore Foodo for this specific dish as of 2026. trans_fat_g set to 0.0: ghee-based preparation with no hydrogenated fats; confirmed negligible trans fat. potassium_mg of 160 mg/100 g is an estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central analogous vegetable biryani composition; HPB does not report potassium for this item. Input record GI value of 15 (low) was rejected as inconsistent with basmati rice-based dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database; replaced with analogous estimate of 56 (medium). saturated_fat_g of 1.8 g/100 g estimated from USDA SR Legacy analogous vegetable biryani entry as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown.
